perturbation.original_text: The Repairer shall be responsible for managing and maintaining the Pool at his own expense and in compliance with the relevant OEM recommendations. Any LRU from the Pool delivered to the Company by the Repairer or any Repair Shop shall comply with the applicable Aircraft technical specifications.
perturbation.changed_text: The Repairer shall endeavor to manage and maintain the Pool at his own expense, in general alignment with OEM recommendations. LRUs from the Pool delivered to the Company by the Repairer or any Repair Shop should aim to meet Aircraft technical specifications.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be responsible' to 'shall endeavor' and 'shall comply' to 'should aim to meet,' the obligation becomes less strict and more open to interpretation. This introduces ambiguity regarding the level of effort and adherence required.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 14 CFR § 145.205 - Maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alterations performed by certificated repair stations
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 14 CFR § 145.205, which requires certificated repair stations to perform maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alterations in accordance with approved technical data and regulatory requirements. By using 'endeavor' and 'aim to meet,' the contract implies that the repairer might not always comply fully with required specifications, creating a contradiction.
perturbation.location: Exhibit 9 - SPARE PARTS STANDARD EXCHANGE SERVICE, Section 3.1 Pool management

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 14 CFR § 145.205 - Maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alterations performed for certificate holders under parts 121, 125, and 135, and for foreign air carriers or foreign persons operating a U.S.-registered aircraft in common carriage under part 129. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 14 CFR § 145.205 - Maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alterations performed for certificate holders under parts 121, 125, and 135, and for foreign air carriers or foreign persons operating a U.S.-registered aircraft in common carriage under part 129. CFR prev | next § 145.205 Maintenance , preventive maintenance , and alterations performed for certificate holders under parts 121, 125, and 135, and for foreign air carriers or foreign persons operating a U.S.-registered aircraft in common carriage under part 129. (a) A certificated repair station that performs maintenance , preventive maintenance , or alterations for an air carrier or commercial operator that has a continuous airworthiness maintenance program under part 121 or part 135 must follow the air carrier 's or commercial operator 's program and applicable sections of its maintenance manual. (b) A certificated repair station that performs inspections for a certificate holder conducting operations under part 125 must follow the operator's FAA -approved inspection program. (c) A certificated repair station that performs maintenance , preventive maintenance , or alterations for a foreign air carrier or foreign person operating a U.S.-registered aircraft under part 129 must follow the operator's FAA -approved maintenance program. (d) The FAA may grant approval for a certificated repair station to perform line maintenance for an air carrier certificated under part 121 or part 135 of this chapter, or a foreign air carrier or foreign person operating a U.S.-registered aircraft in common carriage under part 129 of this chapter on any ai
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates strict compliance with OEM recommendations and aircraft technical specifications ('shall be responsible,' 'shall comply'). The changed text weakens these obligations by using 'endeavor' and 'should aim to meet,' making compliance discretionary. This contradicts 14 CFR § 145.205, as indicated in both scraped law snippets, which requires certificated repair stations to perform maintenance in accordance with regulatory requirements. By suggesting that the repairer 'endeavor' to meet specifications, the modified contract introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance with legally mandated standards.

perturbation.original_text: In such case, the Company shall return to the Repairer such unserviceable Item removed from the Aircraft with a Work Order for repairin the form set out in Exhibit 7-2 within [*****] from the withdrawal of the corresponding Item from the Stock. The Repairer shall then repair, as relevant, such unserviceable Item in accordance with the provisions of Exhibits 10 and/or 11 and re- deliver to the Company a serviceable Item with the relevant associated airworthiness documentation (i.e. a certificate of conformity, ANAC SEGVOO 003 and EASA Form 1 or FAA Form 8130-3,and when applicable the log book duly filled with any technical information). The Company shall then place such serviceable Item into the Stock.
perturbation.changed_text: In such case, the Company should aim to return to the Repairer such unserviceable Item removed from the Aircraft with a Work Order for repair in the form set out in Exhibit 7-2 within a reasonable timeframe from the withdrawal of the corresponding Item from the Stock. The Repairer will endeavor to repair, as relevant, such unserviceable Item in general accordance with the provisions of Exhibits 10 and/or 11 and re-deliver to the Company a serviceable Item, if feasible, with some associated documentation. The Company can then consider placing such a serviceable Item into the Stock.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall return' to 'should aim to return', 'within [*****]' to 'within a reasonable timeframe', 'The Repairer shall then repair... in accordance with the provisions of Exhibits 10 and/or 11' to 'The Repairer will endeavor to repair, as relevant, such unserviceable Item in general accordance with the provisions of Exhibits 10 and/or 11' and 're-deliver to the Company a serviceable Item with the relevant associated airworthiness documentation' to 're-deliver to the Company a serviceable Item, if feasible, with some associated documentation', the obligations are weakened and become more ambiguous. This weakens the compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 14 CFR § 43.13, which requires that each person performing maintenance, alteration, or preventive maintenance on an aircraft, engine, propeller, or appliance shall use the methods, techniques, and practices prescribed in the current manufacturer's maintenance manual or Instructions for Continued Airworthiness prepared by its manufacturer, or other methods, techniques, and practices acceptable to the Administrator. The change makes the adherence and scope of the compliance ambiguous.
perturbation.location: Exhibit 8 - LEASE OF THE STOCK, Section 5. Use and repair of the Stock

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 14 CFR § 43.13 - Performance rules (general). | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 14 CFR § 43.13 - Performance rules (general). CFR prev | next § 43.13 Performance rules (general). (a) Each person performing maintenance , alteration, or preventive maintenance on an aircraft , engine, propeller , or appliance shall use the methods, techniques, and practices prescribed in the current manufacturer's maintenance manual or Instructions for Continued Airworthiness prepared by its manufacturer, or other methods, techniques, and practices acceptable to the Administrator , except as noted in § 43.16 . He shall use the tools, equipment, and test apparatus necessary to assure completion of the work in accordance with accepted industry practices. If special equipment or test apparatus is recommended by the manufacturer involved, he must use that equipment or apparatus or its equivalent acceptable to the Administrator . (b) Each person maintaining or altering, or performing preventive maintenance , shall do that work in such a manner and use materials of such a quality, that the condition of the aircraft , airframe , aircraft engine , propeller , or appliance worked on will be at least equal to its original or properly altered condition (with regard to aerodynamic function, structural strength, resistance to vibration and deterioration, and other qualities affecting airworthiness). (c) Special provisions for holders of air carrier operating certificates and operating certificates issued under the provisions of Part 121 or 135 and Part 129 operators holding operations specifications. Unless otherwise notified by the administrator , the methods, techniques, and practices contained in the maintenance manual or the maintenance part of the manual of the holder of an air carrier operating certificate or an operating certificate under Part 121 or 135 and Part 129 operators holding operations spe
perturbation.justification: The original contract language uses mandatory terms like 'shall' and 'will,' creating clear obligations for both parties. The modified language uses weaker terms like 'should aim to,' 'endeavor to,' and 'if feasible,' making the obligations discretionary and ambiguous. This directly contradicts 14 CFR § 43.13 (Snippet 2), which mandates that maintenance on aircraft be performed using specific methods and techniques. The modified contract weakens the requirement for adherence to specific standards, creating ambiguity where the regulation requires clear compliance. The original text ensures adherence to industry practices by using 'in accordance with the provisions of Exhibits 10 and/or 11', while the changed text uses 'in general accordance with the provisions of Exhibits 10 and/or 11', weakening the standard of compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Within [*****] as from the date of Delivery of any Item of the Stock, the Company shall be responsible for obtaining and shall provide the Repairer with evidence in respect of (i) custom clearance, including payment fees, customs duties, and (ii) customs declarations, with respect to the relevant Stock Item.
perturbation.changed_text: Within a practical timeframe as from the date of Delivery of any Item of the Stock, the Company will try to obtain and provide the Repairer with some evidence regarding (i) custom clearance, including payment fees, customs duties, and (ii) customs declarations, with respect to the relevant Stock Item.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a clear obligation with a deadline. Changing 'Within [*****]' to 'Within a practical timeframe', 'the Company shall be responsible for obtaining and shall provide' to 'the Company will try to obtain and provide' and 'evidence in respect of' to 'some evidence regarding' weakens the obligation and introduces subjectivity, contradicting established import regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change introduces ambiguity that contradicts 19 U.S. Code § 1484, which mandates that the importer of record is responsible for making entry of merchandise, providing necessary documentation, and complying with all customs laws and regulations. The modified language uses 'practical timeframe' and 'try to obtain,' creating uncertainty about the timing and completeness of compliance. The regulation requires a strict compliance instead.
perturbation.location: Exhibit 8 - LEASE OF THE STOCK, Section 3. Stock Delivery, 3.4

Entry of merchandise (a) Requirement and time (1) Except as provided in sections 1490, 1498, 1552, and 1553 of this title , one of the parties qualifying as "importer of record" under paragraph (2)(B), either in person or by an agent authorized by the party in writing, shall, using reasonable care- (A) make entry therefor by filing with the Bureau of Cust
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 19 U.S. Code § 1484 by introducing ambiguity regarding the importer's responsibility for customs compliance. The original text's firm obligation to provide evidence of customs clearance is weakened to 'try to obtain and provide some evidence regarding,' and the specified timeframe is replaced with 'a practical timeframe,' both of which undermine the strict compliance mandated by customs laws. Snippet 1, mentioning 'shall, using reasonable care- (A) make entry therefor by filing with the Bureau of Cust,' highlights the importer's duty to make the entry, which is compromised by the ambiguous wording in the modified contract.
